q: Which theorist's model of light correctly predicted that light slows down in a denser medium?
w: Huygens' wave theory correctly predicted light slows in dense media; Newton's corpuscular theory wrongly predicted it would speed up.
k: Huygens (wave) = light slows in dense media.

q: The four classical theories of light, in order (NHMP), are:
w: Corpuscular (Newton) → Wave (Huygens) → Electromagnetic (Maxwell) → Quantum (Planck).

q: Snell's law is correctly written as:
o: n₁ sin i = n₂ sin R
n₁ cos i = n₂ cos R
w: Snell's law: n₁ sin i = n₂ sin R relates the indices and angles at refraction.
k: n₁ sin i = n₂ sin R.

q: The speed of light in a medium relates to its index by:
w: v = c/n; a higher refractive index means slower light.

q: Diverging light (from a point source) carries what vergence sign?
w: Diverging light = negative vergence; converging light = positive vergence.

q: As refractive index increases, the critical angle:
w: Higher n → lower critical angle. Diamond (~24.44°) has the lowest of common materials.

q: Total internal reflection occurs when the angle of incidence:
o: Is less than the critical angle
w: Going from dense to rare medium, when incidence exceeds the critical angle, all light reflects internally.

q: The keratometer converts radius to power using which index and formula?
w: Keratometers use the keratometric index 1.3375 (not the true 1.376) in D = 337.5/r to account for both corneal surfaces.

q: The cornea provides about what fraction of the eye's total refractive power?
w: The cornea (~43 D) supplies about two-thirds of the eye's ~58.64 D total power.

q: The total power of Gullstrand's exact schematic eye versus the reduced (Emsley) eye is:
w: Gullstrand's exact eye = 58.64 D; the reduced (Emsley) eye = 60 D.

q: How do refractive index and Abbe value relate?
o: Higher index → lower Abbe → more chromatic aberration
Higher index → higher Abbe → less chromatic aberration
w: Index and Abbe value move oppositely: higher index means lower Abbe and more chromatic aberration.
k: Higher n → lower Abbe → more CA.

q: Which UV band causes photokeratitis ('welder's flash')?
w: UV-B causes photokeratitis; UV-A penetrates deeper and is linked to cataract.

q: Compared with photochemical damage, thermal radiation damage is:
w: Thermal injury is permanent; photochemical injury is often reversible.

q: In flat transposition, the new sphere is:
w: New Sph = S + C; New Cyl = −C; New Axis ±90°.

q: What is the refractive index of the cornea (Gullstrand)?
w: Cornea n = 1.376; aqueous and vitreous = 1.336; lens nucleus = 1.406.

q: The aqueous and vitreous humor share which refractive index?
w: Both aqueous and vitreous have n = 1.336.

q: Why does the posterior corneal surface have minus power?
o: Index drops across a convex surface
It is concave toward the front
w: Index falls from cornea to aqueous at a forward-convex surface, so the surface diverges light (minus).
k: Posterior cornea: n drops at convex surface → minus.

q: The crystalline lens (unaccommodated) contributes about:
w: The lens adds ~19 D unaccommodated; the cornea ~43 D.

q: The primary nodal point of the eye lies about how far behind the cornea?
w: Primary nodal point ~7.08 mm, secondary ~7.33 mm; the center of rotation is ~13.5 mm.
k: Nodal point ~7.08 mm (≠ 13.5 mm center of rotation).

q: The clinical emmetropic axial length is about:
w: The emmetropic eye is ~24 mm long axially.

q: Vergence (in diopters) for light from a point is calculated by:
w: D = 100/f when f is in centimeters (or 1/f in meters).

q: Higher-index lens materials tend to produce more of which aberration?
w: High index = low Abbe = more chromatic aberration (color fringing).
k: High index materials = more chromatic aberration.

q: The visible spectrum spans approximately:
w: Visible light is ~400–700 nm; below 400 is UV, above 700 is infrared.

q: A 'UV 400' lens blocks:
w: UV 400 blocks all UV below 400 nm, the gold standard for UV protection.

q: The anterior corneal radius of curvature (Gullstrand) is about:
w: Anterior cornea ~7.7 mm; posterior cornea ~6.8 mm. (Do not confuse with nodal point 7.08 mm.)

q: A plane (flat) mirror forms an image that is:
w: Plane mirror images are virtual, upright, same size, and as far behind as the object is in front.
k: Plane mirror = virtual, upright, same size.

q: A convex mirror forms what kind of image, regardless of object position?
w: Convex (diverging) mirrors always produce virtual, upright, minified images (e.g., car side mirrors).
k: Convex mirror = always virtual, upright, minified.

q: The focal length of a concave mirror is:
w: Concave mirrors converge (positive f); convex mirrors diverge (negative f).
k: Concave mirror f positive; convex negative.

q: In the mirror/lens equation, a negative image distance (q) means:
w: q negative = virtual image (behind a mirror or same side as object for a lens). q positive = real.

q: A negative magnification (m) indicates the image is:
w: Negative m = inverted; positive m = upright. The magnitude gives the size.

q: A concave (minus) lens forms what image for any object position?
w: A minus lens always gives a virtual, upright, minified image.
k: Minus lens = always virtual, upright, minified.

q: A convex lens with the object INSIDE the focal length produces:
w: Object inside F of a plus lens gives a virtual, upright, magnified image (a simple magnifier).
k: Plus lens, object inside F = virtual, magnified.

q: With a prism, light bends toward the ___ and the image displaces toward the ___:
w: Light bends toward the base; the image is displaced toward the apex (opposite directions).
k: Light to base, image to apex.

q: Esotropia is corrected with which prism orientation?
w: Esotropia (eye turned in) is corrected with base-out prism; exotropia with base-in.
k: Esotropia → base out; exotropia → base in.

q: One degree of ocular deviation equals about how many prism diopters?
w: 1° ≈ 1.75Δ (a useful conversion between angular and prism-diopter deviation).

q: A plane refractor (flat glass slab) does what to a transmitted ray?
w: A flat slab only displaces the ray laterally; the exit ray is parallel to the entrance ray (no dioptric power).
k: Plane slab = lateral shift, no power.

q: Surface refracting power depends on:
w: D = (n₂ − n₁)/r. With matched media (n₁ = n₂), power is zero regardless of curvature.
k: Surface power needs both radius and Δn.

q: An object submerged in water appears nearer than it is. The apparent depth equals:
w: Apparent depth = actual depth ÷ n (the shallowing effect of denser media).

q: On the lens neutralization (movement) test, a PLUS lens shows:
w: Plus lenses show 'against' movement; minus lenses show 'with' movement.

q: For a curved mirror, the focal length relates to the radius by:
w: For mirrors, f = R/2.

q: A concave mirror with the object beyond the center of curvature produces an image that is:
w: Object beyond C in a concave mirror → real, inverted, minified image (between F and C).
k: Concave mirror, object beyond C = real, inverted, minified.

q: The law of reflection states:
o: Reflected light bends toward the normal
The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection
The angle of incidence is twice the angle of reflection
w: Angle of incidence = angle of reflection, with both rays and the normal coplanar.

q: Why does a contact lens reduce the effective refractive power needed compared with spectacles in high myopia?
o: It is a thicker lens overall
w: At the corneal plane, the vertex-distance effect is eliminated, so a high minus needs less power than at the spectacle plane.
k: CL at corneal plane removes vertex effect.

q: A dentist's mouth mirror magnifies a tooth held just inside its focal length because it is a:
w: A concave mirror with the object inside F gives a virtual, upright, magnified image.
k: Concave mirror, object inside F = magnified virtual image.

q: Exotropia is corrected with which prism?
w: Exotropia (eye turned out) is corrected with base-in prism; esotropia with base-out.

q: An object at the center of curvature (2F) of a concave mirror forms an image that is:
o: Real, inverted, same size, at C
w: Object at C → image at C, real, inverted, same size.
k: Object at C → real, inverted, same size.

q: The mirror and thin-lens equation is:
w: 1/p + 1/q = 1/f (object distance, image distance, focal length).

q: A surface separating two media of equal index (n₁ = n₂) has:
w: With no index difference, the surface bends no light, so power is zero whatever the curvature.

q: Which common lens material has the LOWEST Abbe value (most chromatic aberration)?
w: Polycarbonate has Abbe ~30 (most CA); crown glass ~58.9 (least).

q: Which lens material is the most impact resistant?
w: Polycarbonate is the most impact resistant, the safety/sports lens of choice.

q: Polycarbonate's UV protection is:
o: Present only with a mirror coat
w: Polycarbonate inherently blocks UV below ~385 nm; no extra UV coating is required.

q: Why do photochromic lenses fail to darken inside a car?
o: Car interior light is too dim
w: Photochromics need UV to activate; windshields block UV, so they stay clear while driving.
k: Windshield blocks UV → photochromics don't darken in cars.

q: Among CPF (corning) filters, a higher CPF number means the lens is:
w: Higher CPF number = more filtering = darker. CPF 450 is lightest; CPF 550 darkest.

q: In anisometropia, conventional slab-off prism is ground on the:
w: Conventional slab-off goes on the more minus/less plus lens to create compensating base-up prism at near.
k: Slab-off on the more minus / less plus lens.

q: Prentice's rule for prism induced by decentration is:
w: P = d(cm) × F. The decentration must be in centimeters.

q: Crown glass has which Abbe value (best optical quality)?
w: Crown glass Abbe ~58.9 is the highest among common materials, giving the least chromatic aberration.

q: Which lens material is the lightest (lowest specific gravity)?
w: Trivex (SG ~1.11) is the lightest; polycarbonate ~1.20.

q: A fused bifocal segment requires which type of glass for the segment?
o: Higher index than the major lens
w: The fused segment uses a higher-index 'button' set into the lower-index major lens to add plus power.

q: The notation '+6.00 × 90' on an optical cross means the surface contributes +6.00 in which meridian?
w: Like cylinder notation, '× 90' means the +6.00 power acts in the 180° meridian (perpendicular to the stated axis).

q: Which filter is typically recommended for a retinitis pigmentosa patient?
w: Yellow/amber filters (CPF 511/527) enhance contrast and reduce glare for RP patients.

q: The FBTT (front-toric) bifocal is made in which cylinder form?
w: FBTT uses minus cyl because the cylinder is on the back surface, opposite the front segment.

q: CR-39 plastic has an Abbe value of about:
w: CR-39 Abbe ~58, much higher (better) than polycarbonate's 30.

q: At what vertical prism differential is slab-off (rather than dropping the OC) generally indicated?
w: Differentials above ~1.5Δ usually need slab-off; smaller ones can be managed by dropping the OC.

q: A patient does sustained near work and wants minimal color fringing. Best material:
w: For optical quality in sustained near gaze, the high Abbe value of CR-39 minimizes chromatic aberration.

q: Which describes a UBTT (one-piece, back-toric) bifocal?
o: Cylinder on front; sphere on back
Base curve on the back surface; plus-cyl form
w: In UBTT the base curve is the back surface (−BC) and it uses plus-cyl form; one-piece bifocals include Ultex and Executive.
k: UBTT = base curve on back, plus cyl.

q: The recommended filter approach for achromatopsia is:
w: Achromats are extremely light-sensitive; dark red filters (CPF 550, Wratten #25/#29) cut the cone-overload glare.

q: For an uncoated CR-39 lens (n ≈ 1.50), the reflection loss from both surfaces is about:
w: By Fresnel's equation each surface reflects ~4% for n≈1.5, so both surfaces together lose roughly 8% (anti-reflective coating reduces this).

q: Examples of fused bifocals include:
w: Fused bifocals: Kryptok, FT-28, FT-35. One-piece bifocals: Ultex, Executive.

q: Which statement about polycarbonate is FALSE?
o: It is the most impact resistant
It is used for safety eyewear
It blocks UV without a coating
It has the highest Abbe value
w: Polycarbonate has the LOWEST Abbe value (30), not the highest. The other statements are true.
k: Polycarbonate Abbe is lowest, not highest.

q: Trivex's Abbe value is approximately:
w: Trivex Abbe ~43–45, between polycarbonate (30) and CR-39 (58), with good impact resistance and light weight.

q: SVTT (single-vision toric) lenses are made in which form, with the base curve on which surface?
o: Plus cyl; base curve on front
Minus cyl; base curve on back
w: SVTT uses plus-cyl with the base curve on the front; the back surface is derived (sph + cyl) − BC.
k: SVTT = plus cyl, BC on front.

q: Vertex-distance power correction becomes clinically necessary above what power?
w: Above ±4.00 D, the vertex distance meaningfully changes effective power; below it, spectacle ≈ CL power.

q: A −6.50 D spectacle Rx at 14 mm vertex converts to a contact lens power of about:
w: D_CL = D_spec/(1 − d·D_spec) = −6.50/1.091 ≈ −5.96 → −6.00 D. A high minus needs less minus at the cornea.
k: High minus → less minus as a CL.

q: Silicone hydrogel contact lenses transmit oxygen primarily through:
w: Silicone hydrogels carry O₂ through the silicone matrix, so high Dk does not require high water content.
k: SiHy O₂ via silicone polymer, not water.

q: Which is TRUE of PMMA contact lenses?
o: They were the first soft lenses
w: PMMA is hard and non-gas-permeable. RGP lenses use different oxygen-permeable polymers.

q: A steep-fitting contact lens tends to cause:
o: Too little movement → hypoxia and edema
w: Steep fit restricts movement and tear exchange → hypoxia/edema. Flat fit causes excess movement and mechanical trauma.

q: The most common organism in contact-lens-related microbial keratitis is:
w: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common bacterial cause; Acanthamoeba is associated with tap water/swimming and extended wear.
k: Pseudomonas = most common CL keratitis organism.

q: Acanthamoeba keratitis is most associated with:
o: Tap water exposure in lens wear
w: Acanthamoeba is linked to extended wear and water exposure (tap water rinsing, swimming). It is very hard to treat.

q: Polymegethism (variable endothelial cell size from chronic CL hypoxia) is:
w: Polymegethism is a permanent endothelial change marking chronic hypoxic stress, even after stopping lens wear.

q: In monovision contact lens fitting, the dominant eye is corrected for:
w: The dominant eye gets distance correction; the non-dominant eye is set for near.

q: Orthokeratology (ortho-K) works by:
w: Ortho-K reshapes the cornea temporarily with rigid lenses worn overnight and helps control myopia progression.
k: Ortho-K = temporary corneal reshaping + myopia control.

q: What is the approximate upper limit of myopia treatable with ortho-K?
w: Ortho-K is generally effective up to about −6.00 D of myopia and −2.00 D of astigmatism.

q: Why should benzalkonium chloride (BAK) never be used with soft contact lenses?
o: It has no effect on lenses
It binds the lens and is toxic
w: BAK is epithelial-toxic and accumulates in soft lens material, so it is contraindicated with soft CLs.
k: BAK is toxic with soft lenses.

q: Giant papillary conjunctivitis (GPC) in lens wearers is caused by:
w: GPC is an immune/mechanical reaction to deposits and friction on the upper tarsus, seen with both soft and RGP wear.

q: A soft contact lens diameter is typically chosen relative to the HVID (~11.7 mm) as:
w: Soft lens total diameter ≈ HVID + 2 mm so the lens vaults onto the sclera for stability.

q: For toric soft lens stabilization, prism ballast is typically:
w: Prism ballast of ~1–1.5Δ base-down uses gravity/lid forces to keep the toric lens oriented.

q: Who developed the first soft (HEMA) contact lens?
w: Otto Wichterle developed HEMA soft lenses; FDA approval came in 1971. Kevin Touhy made the first PMMA corneal lens (1947).

q: A white corneal infiltrate with fluorescein staining and pain in a CL wearer is, until proven otherwise:
w: An infiltrate plus staining and pain is microbial keratitis, a sight-threatening emergency requiring lens removal and referral.

q: Daily disposable lenses were introduced mainly to address:
w: Daily disposables solve poor cleaning compliance by discarding the lens each day, improving hygiene.

q: For a low astigmatic prescription (cyl < 1.00 D) fitted with a spherical soft lens, the power is set using:
w: For small cylinders, the spherical equivalent (sph + ½ cyl), rounded to 0.25 D, is used.
k: Low cyl → spherical equivalent (sph + ½ cyl).

q: Compared with soft lenses, RGP lenses generally require:
w: RGP success depends on trial-lens fitting and evaluation; soft lenses can often be fit empirically.

q: Corneal edema of 7–14% in a conventional extended-wear hydrogel indicates:
w: 7–14% edema is excessive and reflects hypoxia; daily-wear hydrogel is ~2–7% and silicone-hydrogel EW ~2–4%.

q: Which best describes the flat-fitting RGP lens behavior?
w: A flat fit moves and decenters too much, causing mechanical trauma; a steep fit traps the lens and causes hypoxia.

q: The first PMMA corneal contact lens is credited to:
w: Kevin Touhy patented the first PMMA corneal lens in 1947; Wichterle developed soft HEMA lenses later.

q: The four classical theories of light, in order (NHMP), were proposed by:
w: Newton's corpuscular, Huygens' wave, Maxwell's electromagnetic and Planck's quantum theories, remembered as NHMP.
k: Light theories: Newton, Huygens, Maxwell, Planck.

q: The speed of light in a medium relates to its refractive index by:
w: v = c/n, so a higher index means slower light. This slowing at an interface is what bends the ray and produces refraction.
k: v = c/n; higher n = slower light.

q: Which waveband is responsible for photokeratitis (welder's flash)?
w: UV-B drives photokeratitis, a photochemical and therefore reversible injury that heals in 24–48 hours. Thermal (infrared) damage is the permanent kind.

q: Compared with photochemical radiation damage, thermal damage is:
w: Thermal injury coagulates tissue and is permanent; photochemical injury such as photokeratitis is reversible. This is why IR exposure is the more dangerous occupationally.

q: The total refractive power of the eye is approximately:
w: Gullstrand's schematic eye totals about 58.64 D, of which the cornea supplies roughly 43 D, about two-thirds.

q: An image formed by a concave (minus) lens is always:
w: A minus lens diverges light and can never form a real image of a real object: the image is always virtual, erect and minified.
k: Concave lens: always virtual, erect, minified.

q: The relationship between refractive index and Abbe value is that a higher index gives:
o: A higher Abbe and less chromatic aberration
A lower Abbe and more chromatic aberration
A higher Abbe and more chromatic aberration
w: Index and Abbe run opposite: high-index materials disperse more, so they show more chromatic aberration. This is the trade-off in thinning a strong prescription.
k: Higher n → lower Abbe → more CA.

q: The keratometer converts radius to power using the constant:
w: D = 337.5/r using the keratometric index 1.3375, a fictional index that already allows for the cornea's minus posterior surface.

q: In flat transposition, the new cylinder is:
o: The sum of sphere and cylinder
The same sign as the original
w: Flat transposition: new sphere = S + C, new cylinder = −C, new axis = old axis ± 90°.

q: Which lens material has the LOWEST Abbe value and therefore the most chromatic aberration?
w: Polycarbonate's Abbe value of 30 is the lowest in common use, so it disperses most. Crown glass at 58.9 is the highest, with CR-39 close behind at 58.

q: The lightest common spectacle lens material by specific gravity is:
w: Trivex has a specific gravity of 1.11 against polycarbonate's 1.20, so it is the lightest, while matching polycarbonate's impact resistance.

q: The most impact-resistant spectacle lens material is:
w: Polycarbonate is the standard for safety, sports and monocular patients, and blocks UV below 385 nm without any coating.

q: Photochromic lenses darken because of:
o: Silver halide crystals reacting to UV
w: UV drives silver halide crystals to darken. Since a windshield blocks UV, the lenses stay clear inside a car.

q: 'UV 400' on a lens means it blocks all ultraviolet below:
w: UV 400 blocks the full UV spectrum up to 400 nm and is the accepted gold standard. Untreated polycarbonate blocks only to 385 nm.

q: A slab-off is prescribed to correct:
w: Anisometropia produces unequal vertical prism at near. Slab-off removes base-down prism from the more minus lens to rebalance the reading position.
k: Slab-off = vertical prism imbalance in anisometropia.

q: A fused bifocal such as an FT-28 or Kryptok is made in which cylinder form?
w: Fused bifocals (Kryptok, FT-28, FT-35) are front base toric, made in minus cylinder with the toric on the back. One-piece designs like Ultex and Executive are UBTT.

q: The recommended absorptive filter for retinitis pigmentosa is:
w: Amber filters (CPF 511/527) cut short-wavelength scatter and help RP, macular disease and glaucoma. CPF 550 or Wratten #25/#29 dark red suits achromatopsia.
k: RP = amber CPF 511/527; achromatopsia = dark red.

q: The first corneal PMMA contact lens is credited to:
w: Kevin Touhy patented the corneal PMMA lens in 1947. Otto Wichterle later developed the HEMA soft lens, FDA-approved in 1971.

q: Soft contact lenses received FDA approval in:
w: HEMA soft lenses were approved in 1971; disposables followed in 1987, driven by hygiene rather than optics.

q: Silicone hydrogel lenses improve oxygen delivery because oxygen passes:
w: In conventional hydrogels oxygen rides through water, so Dk is capped by water content. Silicone carries oxygen through the polymer, decoupling Dk from water and allowing safe extended wear.
k: SiHy: O2 through polymer, not water.

q: Toric soft lenses are commonly stabilized with prism ballast of:
w: About 1–1.5Δ base-down uses gravity and lid action to keep the cylinder axis oriented.

q: The most common organism in contact-lens microbial keratitis is:
w: Pseudomonas is the commonest and most aggressive cause, capable of perforating a cornea within 24–48 hours. Microbial keratitis is the most dangerous lens complication.

q: A steep-fitting RGP lens produces:
w: A steep lens grips the cornea, restricting movement and tear exchange, so debris accumulates and the cornea swells. A flat lens moves too much and causes mechanical trauma.

q: In monovision, the dominant eye is normally corrected for:
w: The dominant eye takes the distance correction and the non-dominant eye the near, which patients tolerate better.

q: Orthokeratology is generally effective up to a myopia of about:
w: Ortho-K reshapes the cornea temporarily and controls myopia progression, but the effect is insufficient beyond about −6.00 D of myopia or −2.00 D of astigmatism.
k: Ortho-K limits: −6.00 D sphere, −2.00 D cyl.

q: Vertex distance correction becomes clinically necessary beyond about:
w: Below ±4.00 D the spectacle and contact lens powers are effectively equal. Above it, use D_CL = D_spec ÷ (1 − 0.012 × D_spec).

q: A soft contact lens diameter is normally chosen as:
w: With a typical HVID of 11.7 mm, a soft lens runs about 14 mm so it vaults the limbus and stays centered.

q: Polymegethism in a long-term lens wearer signifies:
o: Normal aging of the tear film
w: Polymegethism is variation in endothelial cell size, a permanent marker of chronic hypoxia. Unlike edema it does not resolve when lens wear stops.

q: Normal corneal edema for daily-wear hydrogel lenses is:
w: Daily-wear hydrogels give 2–7% edema, and silicone hydrogel extended wear stays at 2–4%. Conventional extended wear at 7–14% is excessive.

q: Which fitting can be done empirically, without a trial lens?
w: Soft lenses can be ordered empirically from K readings and refraction. RGPs must be trial fitted because the fluorescein pattern cannot be predicted reliably.
k: SCL empirical; RGP needs trial fitting.

q: Newton's corpuscular theory was disproved because it predicted that light in a denser medium would:
w: Newton's corpuscular theory predicted light speeds up in a denser medium. Measurement showed the opposite, vindicating Huygens' wave theory: v = c/n, so light slows.
k: Newton predicted speeding up; Huygens (correctly) slowing.

q: As refractive index rises, the critical angle:
w: Index and critical angle move oppositely. Diamond, with the highest common index, has the lowest critical angle at 24.44°, which is why it traps and sparkles with light.

q: Which waveband penetrates deeper and is associated with cataract?
w: UV-B is absorbed by the cornea and causes photokeratitis. UV-A penetrates deeper to the lens and is linked to cataract. Do not swap the two.

q: The keratometric index of 1.3375 is used instead of the cornea's true index because it:
w: The true corneal index is 1.376. The simplified keratometric index 1.3375 folds in the minus posterior surface, so one anterior measurement yields total corneal power via D = 337.5/r.
k: 1.3375 is a fiction that absorbs the posterior surface.

q: Light diverging from a point source carries vergence that is:
w: Diverging light carries negative vergence, converging light positive. Vergence is not always positive; the sign follows the direction of travel relative to the focus.

q: Light passing through a prism bends toward the base, while the image moves toward the:
w: Light deviates toward the base but the image displaces toward the apex: they move in opposite directions. This is why the correcting prism's base points opposite the deviation.
k: Light to the base; image to the apex.

q: The correcting prism for esotropia is:
w: An esotropic eye turns inward, so the base points outward to draw the gaze back out. Exotropia takes base in. Light bends to the base, but the image shifts to the apex.
k: Esotropia = base out; exotropia = base in.

q: On the lens movement test, a plus lens shows:
w: A plus lens shows against movement, where the target travels opposite the lens. A minus lens shows with movement. Students routinely reverse this.

q: A convex mirror forms an image that is:
w: A convex mirror always gives a virtual, upright, minified image whatever the object position, which is why it is used as a wide-view security mirror.
k: Convex mirror: always virtual, upright, minified.

q: An object submerged in water appears:
w: Refraction makes objects in a denser medium appear closer to the observer. Apparent depth = actual depth ÷ n, the shallowing effect that makes pools look shallow.

q: Among CPF absorptive filters, a higher CPF number means the lens is:
w: A higher CPF number filters more and looks darker: CPF 450 is lightest, CPF 550 darkest. So CPF 527 is darker than CPF 450, not lighter.

q: Conventional slab-off is applied to which lens in anisometropia?
w: Slab-off removes base-down prism from the more minus (or less plus) lens, correcting the vertical imbalance at the reading level.
k: Slab-off goes on the more minus lens.

q: In a front base toric (FBTT) lens, the base curve is:
w: In FBTT the BC is on the front (segment side) and must be spherical, leaving the back toric. This is why FBTT is made in minus cylinder form.
k: FBTT: BC front and spherical, back toric, minus cyl.

q: In an ultex base toric (UBTT) lens, which surface is the base curve?
w: In UBTT the BC is the back surface (−BC) and the front is derived. In SVTT it is the reverse: the BC is the front and the back is calculated.
k: UBTT: BC is the back; SVTT: BC is the front.

q: Which lens material has the HIGHER Abbe value?
w: CR-39's Abbe value of 58 is far higher than polycarbonate's 30, so CR-39 shows much less chromatic aberration. Polycarbonate wins on impact resistance, not optics.

q: PMMA is best described as:
w: PMMA transmits no oxygen, which is why it caused corneal edema and has been replaced by RGP materials whose different polymers do permit gas exchange.

q: Giant papillary conjunctivitis occurs in:
w: GPC/CLPC arises from lens deposits plus mechanical friction on the upper tarsus, so it affects both modalities. It is not exclusive to RGP.
k: GPC affects both soft and RGP wearers.

q: Daily disposable lenses were introduced chiefly to address:
w: The driver in 1987 was hygiene: patients did not clean lenses reliably, so discarding them daily removed the deposit and solution problem altogether.

q: For a −8.00 D spectacle prescription, the contact lens power should be:
w: Moving a minus lens to the corneal plane reduces the minus needed: D_CL = D_spec ÷ (1 − 0.012 × D_spec) gives about −7.30 D. High plus needs MORE plus at the cornea. Correction applies above ±4.00 D.
k: High minus: less minus at the cornea.

q: Compared with a steep-fitting lens, a flat-fitting lens causes harm mainly by:
w: Both fits harm the cornea by different routes: flat causes mechanical trauma from excessive movement, steep causes hypoxia from restricted tear exchange. Neither is the safer error.

q: A patient's spectacle prescription is OD −3.00 −1.00 × 90. The patient's pupillary distance is 30mm from the OC of the right lens. What is the prismatic effect?
w: Using Prentice's rule: P = d × F = 3.0 cm × ... Wait — the question asks about a sphero-cylindrical lens. The prismatic effect at a given point depends on the meridional power in the direction of decentration. If the 3mm decentration is horizontal (temporal), we use the horizontal meridian power. For −3.00 −1.00 × 90: horizontal meridian (180°) = sphere = −3.00 D. P = 0.3 cm × 3.00 = 0.9Δ. For a minus lens, the prism base is AWAY from the optical center (toward the apex in the direction of displacement). With temporal decentration on OD, the base is temporal (outward) for plus lenses but for minus lenses the base is nasal (inward). Answer: 0.9Δ base in (nasal = toward nose). (Note: verify meridional power direction in exam context.)
k: Using Prentice's rule: P = d × F = 3.0 cm × ...

q: A ray of light travels from crown glass (n = 1.523) into air at an angle of incidence of 50°. What happens?
o: Light refracts normally and bends away from the normal
Light refracts and bends toward the normal
w: The critical angle for crown glass → air is 41.04° (sin CA = 1.0/1.523). Since the angle of incidence (50°) exceeds the critical angle (41.04°), no light is transmitted into the air — total internal reflection occurs. This principle is applied in retinoscopy, prism design, and fiber optics.04°.
k: The critical angle for crown glass → air is 41.04° (sin CA = 1.0/1.523).

q: A lens has a front surface power of +10.00D and a back surface power of −4.00D. Center thickness is 6 mm and index is 1.5. A lensometer is used with the concave side against the lens stop. Which value does the lensometer display?
w: The lensometer with the concave (back) surface against the lens stop reads back vertex power (BVP). BVP = D₁ + D₂ + (t/n)(D₁)² = +10.00 + (−4.00) + (0.006/1.5)(+10.00)² = +6.00 + 0.004 × 100 = +6.00 + 0.40 = +6.40D. (Note: rounding differences possible by exam — the key concept is that BVP > nominal power for plus lenses, and lensometer with concave side down = BVP.) Nominal power = D₁ + D₂ = +6.00D only applies to thin lenses. Equivalent power (Deq) subtracts the interaction term. FVP is read when the convex side is placed down.
k: The lensometer with the concave (back) surface against the lens stop reads back vertex power (BVP).

q: A dentist uses a concave mirror with radius of curvature 40mm to examine a tooth positioned 15mm from the mirror. What type of image is formed and what is the magnification?
w: F = R/2 = 40/2 = 20mm. Object at 15mm = inside the focal length (15 < 20). When the object is between F and the mirror, the concave mirror produces a virtual, upright, magnified image behind the mirror (same as a magnifying mirror). Using the formula: 1/15 + 1/q = 1/20 → 1/q = 1/20 − 1/15 = 3/60 − 4/60 = −1/60 → q = −60mm (negative = virtual, behind mirror). M = −q/p = −(−60)/15 = +4. Positive m = upright; |m| = 4 = magnified 4×. This is exactly how dentist's mouth mirrors work — they are concave mirrors used with the tooth just inside the focal length to produce a magnified virtual image.

q: A prism is prescribed for a patient with 8△ of right exotropia. The correct prism for the right eye is:
w: Exotropia means the right eye deviates outward (temporally). To correct it, the prism must redirect the visual axis inward (nasally). A Base In prism has its apex temporal — light bends toward the base (nasal), and the eye follows the image toward the apex (temporal). Wait — re-reading: image displaces toward APEX. Apex is temporal → image appears temporal → eye moves toward apex (temporal). That would increase the exotropia. Let me reconsider. Correction: For exotropia (eye turns OUT), the correcting prism is Base IN. Here's the reasoning: the prism must move the image nasally (inward) so the eye moves inward to fixate it. With Base IN, the base faces nasally — light bends toward the nasal base — the image displaces toward the temporal APEX. The eye follows TOWARD THE APEX (temporal)... This seems wrong. Clarification: The rule is — the CORRECTING prism base points toward the deviated direction. Right exotropia = eye deviated OUT (temporal). Correcting prism base points INWARD (nasal) = Base IN. This places the base between the deviated eye and the image, bending light toward it. Answer: B (Base In right eye) or D (split equally). The Board most commonly asks for the full prism on the affected eye: B.
k: Exotropia means the right eye deviates outward (temporally).

q: A coin sits 40cm below the surface of water (n=1.33). Where does the coin appear to an observer directly above the water?
w: Using the shallowing formula for an observer in air (n' = 1.0): q = p × (n'/n) = 40 × (1.0/1.33) = 40/1.33 = 30.08 ≈ 30 cm. The coin appears 30cm deep, not 40cm. This is the shallowing effect — objects in denser media always appear closer to the observer due to refraction at the surface. The ratio 1/n = 1/1.33 = 0.75 means objects in water appear at 75% of their actual depth. This is also why pools appear shallower than they are, and why slit-lamp depth measurements must be corrected by dividing by the medium's index.
k: Using the shallowing formula for an observer in air (n' = 1.0): q = p × (n'/n) = 40 × (1.0/1.33) = 40/1.33 =…

q: A 35-year-old graphic designer spends 10 hours daily reading fine print on a computer. He is myopic (−2.50 D OU) and wants thin lenses. Which lens material would you prioritize?
w: For a sedentary professional spending hours reading through lenses — especially at a monitor where off-axis gaze is constant — optical quality (Abbe value) is the priority. CR-39 has Abbe=58, producing minimal color fringing even in peripheral gaze. While polycarbonate would be thinner, its Abbe=30 produces significant color aberration during sustained near work, causing visual fatigue. High-index glass is thinner but has lower Abbe (36) and is heavier. Trivex (Abbe 43-45) is a reasonable compromise but costs more without significant advantage here. For −2.50 D, the lens is already relatively thin in CR-39. Safety is not a primary concern for this patient in a seated work environment.
k: For a sedentary professional spending hours reading through lenses — especially at a monitor where off-axis…

q: A patient with anisometropia (OD +1.00D, OS +4.50D) looks down 8mm below the optical centers while reading. What is the vertical prism differential at the reading level, and which method should be used to correct it?
w: Using Prentice's rule for each eye at 8mm (0.8cm) below OC: OD prism = 0.8 × 1.00 = 0.8Δ BU; OS prism = 0.8 × 4.50 = 3.6Δ BU. Differential = 3.6 − 0.8 = 2.8Δ. Since 2.8Δ > 1.5Δ, slab-off is indicated. For two plus lenses, slab-off is ground on the MOST PLUS lens (OS = +4.50). Slab-off creates base-up prism in the reading zone to neutralize the differential. Drop OC is only used for differentials ≤ 1.5Δ.
k: Using Prentice's rule for each eye at 8mm (0.8cm) below OC: OD prism = 0.8 × 1.00 = 0.8Δ BU; OS prism = 0.8 ×…

q: A spectacle lens (CR-39, n = 1.498) is uncoated. What percentage of light is lost to reflection from both surfaces?
w: Using the reflectance formula: [(n' − n)/(n' + n)]² × 100 = [(1.498 − 1)/(1.498 + 1)]² × 100 = (0.498/2.498)² × 100 ≈ 3.87% per surface. For an uncoated lens, both the front and back surfaces reflect equally: 3.87% × 2 = 7.75% total light lost. This leaves ~92.25% transmitted. 3.87% is per-surface only. 4.45% is the loss with single-coat AR (front uncoated + back 85% reduced). 0.58% is the back surface reflection with a single coat (15% of 3.87%).

q: A 28-year-old patient wearing conventional hydrogel lenses (monthly replacement) presents with red eye, foreign body sensation, and reduced vision after wearing lenses for 12 hours. Slit-lamp reveals corneal edema and a white infiltrate with fluorescein staining. The most urgent concern is:
w: A white infiltrate with fluorescein staining in a CL wearer is microbial keratitis until proven otherwise — this is the most serious CL complication and a potential sight-threatening emergency. The combination of pain, infiltrate, and staining requires immediate removal of the lens and urgent referral to an ophthalmologist for culture, scraping, and topical antibiotics. Extended or continuous wear, poor hygiene, and swimming with lenses are major risk factors. Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common organism in CL-related infectious keratitis. GPC presents as cobblestone papillae under the upper lid (not infiltrate). Neovascularization is asymptomatic initially. Abrasion would not typically present with a white infiltrate.
k: A white infiltrate with fluorescein staining in a CL wearer is microbial keratitis until proven otherwise…

q: A patient's spectacle refraction is −6.50D at a vertex distance of 14mm. What is the appropriate contact lens power to prescribe?
w: Using the vertex distance formula: D_CL = D_Spec / (1 − d × D_Spec) = −6.50 / (1 − 0.014 × (−6.50)) = −6.50 / (1 + 0.091) = −6.50 / 1.091 = −5.96D → round to −6.00D. The vertex distance effect is significant here because |Rx| > 4.00D. Moving a minus lens from 14mm (spectacle plane) to 0mm (corneal plane) reduces the effective minus power needed. If you prescribed the same −6.50D as a CL, the patient would be overcorrected (too much minus at the cornea).
k: Using the vertex distance formula: D_CL = D_Spec / (1 − d × D_Spec) = −6.50 / (1 − 0.014 × (−6.50)) = −6.50 /…

q: A patient's keratometry reads 43.00 / 45.00 @ 90. Spectacle refraction is −2.00 −3.00 × 180. What is the residual astigmatism if an RGP spherical lens is fitted?
w: Residual astigmatism = Total astigmatism − Corneal astigmatism. Total astigmatism (from spectacle Rx) = −3.00D. Corneal astigmatism (from K readings) = 45.00 − 43.00 = 2.00D. Residual = 3.00 − 2.00 = 1.00D. This residual 1.00D is mostly lenticular (internal) astigmatism — the RGP tear lens corrects the 2.00D corneal component but cannot touch the lenticular component. Since residual > 0.75D, a toric RGP or toric SCL would be needed for complete correction. 0.00 would only apply if total = corneal astigmatism (purely corneal origin).
k: Residual astigmatism = Total astigmatism − Corneal astigmatism.

q: In the mirror and lens formula, a POSITIVE image distance q indicates:
w: Using 1/p + 1/q = 1/f, a positive q means a real image: in front of a mirror, or behind a lens. A negative q means virtual. Sign, not size, is what q reports.
k: q positive = real; q negative = virtual.

q: A NEGATIVE magnification m indicates an image that is:
w: The sign of m gives orientation, not size: negative is inverted, positive is upright. The magnitude of m gives the size.
k: m negative = inverted; m positive = upright.

q: The focal length of a convex mirror is:
w: A convex mirror diverges, so f is negative and the image is always virtual, upright and minified. A concave mirror converges and has a positive f.
k: Convex mirror f negative; concave f positive.

q: One degree of deviation equals how many prism dioptres?
w: 1° ≈ 1.75Δ. The reciprocal is also worth knowing: 1Δ ≈ 0.57°. Board questions convert between the two.

q: The laws of reflection state that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, and that:
o: The reflected ray bends toward the normal
The ray speeds up on reflection
Incident ray, normal and reflected ray are coplanar
w: Reflection needs both conditions: i = r, and all three of incident ray, normal and reflected ray lying in one plane. Reflection does not change the medium, so speed is unchanged.
k: Reflection: i = r, and all three coplanar.

q: Why does a contact lens reduce the cornea's contribution to refractive power?
o: It removes the air-to-cornea index step
It absorbs part of the incident light
It sits closer to the nodal point
w: The tear layer under the lens abolishes the large air-cornea index jump that supplies most of the cornea's 43 D. The step is re-created at the contact lens front surface instead.
k: CL removes the air-cornea index step.

q: The refractive index of the cornea is:
w: The cornea's true index is 1.376. Aqueous and vitreous both sit at 1.336, the lens cortex at 1.386 and the nucleus at 1.406. The keratometer's 1.3375 is a separate, simplified fiction.

q: The refractive index of the lens NUCLEUS is:
w: The nucleus is 1.406 and the cortex 1.386. The gradient of index from cortex to nucleus is what lets the lens outperform a homogeneous lens of the same shape.

q: The radius of curvature of the anterior cornea is:
w: Anterior cornea 7.7 mm, posterior cornea 6.8 mm. The unaccommodated lens runs 10.0 mm anteriorly and 6.0 mm posteriorly, steepening to 5.33 mm anteriorly on full accommodation.
k: Anterior cornea r = 7.7 mm; posterior 6.8 mm.

q: On full accommodation, the anterior lens radius steepens from 10.0 mm to:
w: The anterior lens surface steepens from 10.0 mm to 5.33 mm, which is where most of the accommodative power change comes from. The posterior surface barely moves.

q: The unaccommodated crystalline lens contributes how much power in Gullstrand's schematic eye?
w: Gullstrand gives the lens 19 D unaccommodated against the cornea's 43 D, totalling 58.64 D for the eye. Emsley's reduced eye simplifies this to a round 60 D.
k: Gullstrand: cornea 43 D, lens 19 D, total 58.64 D.

q: Emsley's REDUCED schematic eye assigns the eye a total power of:
w: The reduced eye rounds to 60 D with a single refracting surface, which makes calculation tractable. Gullstrand's exact eye keeps the true 58.64 D across multiple surfaces.
k: Reduced eye 60 D; Gullstrand exact 58.64 D.

q: The eye's center of rotation lies approximately how far behind the corneal apex?
w: The center of rotation sits about 13.5 mm behind the corneal apex. The nodal points are far more anterior at 7.08 mm and 7.33 mm.
k: Centre of rotation 13.5 mm; nodal points 7.08/7.33 mm.

q: The primary and secondary nodal points of the eye lie at:
w: N sits at 7.08 mm and N' at 7.33 mm behind the corneal apex. A ray aimed at the nodal point emerges undeviated, which is what makes them the reference for angle alpha.
k: Nodal points: N 7.08 mm, N' 7.33 mm.

q: Which term describes n₁ sin i = n₂ sin R; governs bending at every optical interface?
w: Snell's Law: Named after Willebrord Snell. n₁ sin i = n₂ sin R; governs bending at every optical interface
k: Snell's Law = n₁ sin i = n₂ sin R; governs bending at every optical interface

q: Which term describes light going dense→rare beyond critical angle is fully reflected?
w: TIR: Total Internal Reflection. Light going dense→rare beyond critical angle is fully reflected
k: TIR = Light going dense→rare beyond critical angle is fully reflected

q: Which term describes unit of vergence and lens power = 1/f (meters)?
w: Diopter: Named after Greek dioptron. Unit of vergence and lens power = 1/f (meters)
k: Diopter = Unit of vergence and lens power = 1/f (meters)

q: Which term describes prismatic effect P = d × F; d = distance from OC in cm, F = power in D?
w: Prentice's Rule: Named after Charles Prentice. Prismatic effect P = d × F; d = distance from OC in cm, F = power in D
k: Prentice's Rule = Prismatic effect P = d × F; d = distance from OC in cm, F = power in D

q: Which term describes measure of chromatic aberration; higher = less dispersion = better optical quality?
w: Abbe value: Named after Ernst Abbe. Measure of chromatic aberration; higher = less dispersion = better optical quality
k: Abbe value = Measure of chromatic aberration; higher = less dispersion = better optical quality

q: The CR-39 n is:
w: CR-39 n: 1.498. Standard plastic; good CA control

q: The polycarbonate n is:
w: Polycarbonate n: 1.586. Most impact resistant

q: The polycarbonate SG is:
w: Polycarbonate SG: 1.20. Lightest manufactured lens

q: The trivex n is:
w: Trivex n: 1.532. Best for drill-mount (rigid)

q: The trivex Abbe is:
w: Trivex Abbe: 43–45. Better than polycarbonate

q: The crown glass Abbe is:
w: Crown glass Abbe: 58.9. Best CA control in glass

q: The CR-39 Abbe is:
w: CR-39 Abbe: 58. Best CA control in plastic

q: Which term describes lens that darkens on UV exposure via silver halide reaction?
w: Photochromic: Greek photos + chroma. Lens that darkens on UV exposure via silver halide reaction
k: Photochromic = Lens that darkens on UV exposure via silver halide reaction

q: Which term describes longpass filter for low vision patients?
w: CPF filter: Corning Contrast Preservation Filter. Longpass filter for low vision patients
k: CPF filter = Longpass filter for low vision patients

q: Which term describes gelatin/glass trial filter for low vision assessment?
w: Wratten filter: Named after Frederick Wratten. Gelatin/glass trial filter for low vision assessment
k: Wratten filter = Gelatin/glass trial filter for low vision assessment

q: The first soft CL FDA approval is:
w: First soft CL FDA approval: 1971. Hydrogel CL mainstream availability

q: The first disposable CL is:
w: First disposable CL: 1987. Hygiene improvement

q: The first silicone hydrogel is:
w: First silicone hydrogel: 1999–2002. O2 breakthrough

q: The SCL diameter is:
w: SCL diameter: 13.5–15 mm. Covers limbus

q: The RGP diameter is:
w: RGP diameter: 8–10 mm. Corneal only

q: The SCL typical BC is:
w: SCL typical BC: Flatter than K by ~0.8–1.0 mm. Starting point for empirical fit
k: SCL typical BC = Flatter than K by ~0.8–1.0 mm

q: The tear film pH is:
w: Tear film pH: 7.4. CL solution target range

q: The CL solution pH range is:
w: CL solution pH range: 6.5–8.0. Outside = burn risk

q: The HVID is:
w: HVID: 11.7 mm average. Horizontal visible iris diameter; SCL diameter = HVID + ~2 mm

q: The VVID is:
w: VVID: 10.6 mm average. Vertical visible iris diameter

q: The vertex distance correction threshold is:
w: Vertex distance correction threshold: > ±4.00 D. Below this, CL power = spectacle power

q: The spherical equivalent rule is:
w: Spherical equivalent rule: D_CL = Sph + ½ Cyl (if cyl < 1.00 D). "4:1 rule"; round to nearest 0.25 D
k: Spherical equivalent rule = D_CL = Sph + ½ Cyl (if cyl < 1.00 D)

q: The SCL BOZR selection is:
w: SCL BOZR selection: Flattest K + 0.3–0.9 mm. Starting point; steepen if steep fit, flatten if flat fit
k: SCL BOZR selection = Flattest K + 0.3–0.9 mm

q: The soft lens TD/BOZR rule is:
w: Soft lens TD/BOZR rule: ΔTD 0.5 mm = ΔBOZR 0.3 mm. Change diameter → compensate BOZR
k: Soft lens TD/BOZR rule = ΔTD 0.5 mm = ΔBOZR 0.3 mm

q: The ortho-K max myopia is:
w: Ortho-K max myopia: −6.00 D. Beyond this, insufficient corneal flattening

q: The ortho-K max astigmatism is:
w: Ortho-K max astigmatism: −2.00 D. Irregular astigmatism develops above this

q: Which term describes dk ÷ lens thickness (t); the actual oxygen delivery to the cornea?
w: Dk/t: Oxygen transmissibility. Dk ÷ lens thickness (t); the actual oxygen delivery to the cornea
k: Dk/t = Dk ÷ lens thickness (t); the actual oxygen delivery to the cornea

q: Which term describes must approximate the corneal curvature for proper fit?
w: Base curve (BC): Radius of curvature of the back surface. Must approximate the corneal curvature for proper fit
k: Base curve (BC) = Must approximate the corneal curvature for proper fit

q: Which term describes monomer developed by Wichterle; basis of all hydrogel soft CL?
w: HEMA: Hydroxyethyl methacrylate. Monomer developed by Wichterle; basis of all hydrogel soft CL
k: HEMA = Monomer developed by Wichterle; basis of all hydrogel soft CL

q: Which term describes first plastic CL material; non-gas permeable; Kevin Touhy (1947)?
w: PMMA: Polymethyl methacrylate. First plastic CL material; non-gas permeable; Kevin Touhy (1947)
k: PMMA = First plastic CL material; non-gas permeable; Kevin Touhy (1947)

q: Which term describes hard lens that transmits oxygen through the polymer?
w: RGP: Rigid Gas Permeable. Hard lens that transmits oxygen through the polymer
k: RGP = Hard lens that transmits oxygen through the polymer

q: Which term describes overnight wear of specially designed RGP to temporarily reshape cornea; corrects myopia?
w: Ortho-K: Orthokeratology. Overnight wear of specially designed RGP to temporarily reshape cornea; corrects myopia
k: Ortho-K = Overnight wear of specially designed RGP to temporarily reshape cornea; corrects myopia

q: Ametropia arising from normal biological variation, largely hereditary and generally under 6.00 D, is termed:
w: Correlative (simple) ametropia comes from normal variation in otherwise normal components, runs about 0.25 to 6.00 D and is not progressive beyond normal growth. Component (pathological) ametropia comes from abnormal development or disease, can exceed 6.00 D and can keep progressing.
k: Correlative = simple, <6 D, normal variation; component = pathological, >6 D.

q: Ametropia due to abnormal development or acquired disease, able to progress beyond 6.00 D, is termed:
w: Component ametropia is the pathological form: one ocular component is genuinely abnormal, so the error can exceed 6.00 D and progress. Correlative ametropia is normal variation and stays under about 6.00 D.
k: Component = pathological, can exceed 6 D and progress.

q: Axial length increases by roughly how much from birth to age 3?
w: The globe grows about 5 mm in the first three years, the fastest phase, then about 1 mm per year from 3 to 13. This rapid early growth is what emmetropization has to track.
k: Axial length: +5 mm birth to 3; ~1 mm/yr from 3 to 13.

q: Between ages 3 and 13, axial length increases by approximately:
w: About 1 mm per year from 3 to 13, against roughly 5 mm total in the first three years. The globe grows most rapidly pre-school.

q: Aniseikonia in which one ocular image is larger in a single meridian only is called:
w: Meridional symmetrical aniseikonia enlarges one meridian, usually vertical or horizontal, and typically follows meridional (cylindrical) correction. Overall symmetrical enlarges all dimensions equally; compound combines the two.
k: Meridional = one meridian only; overall = all dimensions.

q: Aniseikonia in which one image is symmetrically larger in ALL dimensions is called:
w: Overall symmetrical aniseikonia scales the whole image and typically follows spherical anisometropia. Meridional affects one meridian; compound is a combination of both.
k: Overall symmetrical = image scaled in every dimension.
